subject: English
title: Extended Reading Passages with Questions – A Letter to God
instructions: Read each passage carefully. Answer the questions using information only from the given paragraph.

passages.paragraph: It was a clear day in the village, and the sun shone brightly over the fields. Lencho, a poor farmer, looked at his land with hope. He had worked tirelessly all year, planting corn, beans, and vegetables. The soil was rich, and the plants were growing strong. Lencho prayed silently, wishing for rain to help his crops flourish.
questions: What is the main idea of this paragraph?
Who is Lencho and what is he doing?
Why is rain important to the farmer?
Identify words that show Lencho’s hopefulness.
How does this paragraph set the scene for the story?

passages.paragraph: Suddenly, dark clouds appeared on the horizon, and the wind began to blow. Lencho watched nervously as the first drops of rain fell on his fields. At first, it seemed like a blessing, but soon, hailstones began to crash down. The young plants were battered and broken, and the hard work of months lay in ruins. Lencho felt a wave of despair but continued to pray to God for help.
questions: What change occurs in the weather?
How do the hailstones affect the crops?
How does Lencho react to the destruction?
Which words show the farmer’s despair?
What does this paragraph tell you about the theme of faith?

passages.paragraph: After the storm, the field was white as if covered with salt. Not a leaf remained on the trees. The corn was totally destroyed. Lencho’s soul was filled with sadness. When the storm had passed, he stood in the middle of the field and told his sons that even a plague of locusts would have left more than this.
questions: What did the field look like after the storm?
What was the condition of the corn?
How did Lencho feel once the storm passed?
What comparison does Lencho make regarding the destruction?
What does the phrase 'covered with salt' signify?

passages.paragraph: But in the hearts of all who lived in that solitary house in the middle of the valley, there was a single hope: help from God. Lencho comforted his family by saying that no one dies of hunger. He spent the entire night thinking only of his one hope, the help of God, whose eyes see everything, even what is deep in one’s conscience.
questions: Where was Lencho’s house located?
What was the 'single hope' shared by the family?
How did Lencho try to reassure his family?
What does Lencho believe about God’s sight?
Identify a word that means 'lonely' or 'isolated'.

passages.paragraph: Lencho was an ox of a man, working like an animal in the fields, but still he knew how to write. At daybreak on Sunday, he began to write a letter which he himself would carry to town and place in the mail. It was nothing less than a letter to God.
questions: What does the phrase 'ox of a man' imply about Lencho?
What surprising skill did this farmer possess?
When did he start writing the letter?
Where did he plan to take the letter?
To whom was the letter addressed?

passages.paragraph: “God,” he wrote, “if you don’t help me, my family and I will go hungry this year. I need a hundred pesos in order to sow my field again and to live until the crop comes, because the hailstorm...” He wrote ‘To God’ on the envelope, put the letter inside and, still troubled, went to town.
questions: What reason did Lencho give God for needing help?
How much money did he request?
Why did he need this specific amount?
What was written on the envelope?
What was Lencho's state of mind as he went to town?

passages.paragraph: One of the employees, who was a postman and also helped at the post office, went to his boss laughing heartily and showed him the letter to God. Never in his career as a postman had he known that address. The postmaster—a fat, amiable fellow—also broke out laughing, but almost immediately he turned serious.
questions: Who first discovered Lencho's letter?
Why was the postman laughing?
How is the postmaster described in the passage?
What was the postmaster's immediate reaction after laughing?
What does 'amiable' mean in this context?

passages.paragraph: The postmaster was moved by the writer's faith. “What faith! I wish I had the faith of the man who wrote this letter,” he thought. So, in order not to shake the writer’s faith in God, the postmaster came up with an idea: answer the letter. But when he opened it, it was evident that to answer it he needed something more than goodwill, ink and paper.
questions: What quality in Lencho impressed the postmaster?
Why did the postmaster decide to answer the letter?
What did the postmaster realize upon opening the letter?
Does the postmaster admire or mock Lencho in this paragraph?
What does the phrase 'shake the writer’s faith' mean?

passages.paragraph: He asked for money from his employees, he himself gave part of his salary, and several friends of his were obliged to give something ‘for an act of charity.’ It was impossible for him to gather together the hundred pesos, so he was able to send the farmer only a little more than half. He put the money in an envelope addressed to Lencho and with it a letter containing only a single word as a signature: God.
questions: How did the postmaster collect the money?
Was he able to collect the full amount requested?
How much money was he able to send?
What was the only word written in the reply letter?
Why do you think he signed the letter as 'God'?

passages.paragraph: Lencho showed not the slightest surprise on seeing the money; such was his confidence. But he became angry when he counted the money. God could not have made a mistake, nor could he have denied Lencho what he had requested. Immediately, Lencho went up to the window to ask for paper and ink.
questions: Why was Lencho not surprised to see the money?
What made Lencho angry?
What two things was Lencho certain God would not do?
What did Lencho do immediately after counting the money?
What does this paragraph reveal about Lencho's level of faith?
